section.title: Nehemiah's Prayer
section.sectionRationale: The book opens far from Jerusalem, in the Persian citadel of Susa, where Nehemiah the cupbearer hears that the survivors of the exile are in disgrace and the wall of the holy city lies broken and burned. The news undoes him. Before a single stone is laid, Nehemiah sits, weeps, fasts, and prays, and the prayer he prays sets the tone for the whole book: he addresses the covenant-keeping God of heaven, confesses the nation's sin as his own, pleads God's own promise to Moses, and asks for success before the king. It is one movement in which grief becomes intercession.
section.thesis: Before he lifts a tool, Nehemiah lifts a prayer: hearing of ruin, he casts himself on the great and awesome God who keeps covenant loving devotion, owning his people's sin, appealing to God's own word, and asking that a merciful God would move a pagan king, so that the rebuilding begins on its knees.
section.frameNote: Three beats: the report from a broken Jerusalem and Nehemiah's grief, verses 1 to 4; the address and confession before the covenant God, verses 5 to 7; and the appeal to God's promise and the plea for success, verses 8 to 11.

units.label: News from a broken city
units.note: the wall is broken down, the gates burned

verses.text: These are the words of Nehemiah son of Hacaliah: In the month of Chislev, in the twentieth year, while I was in the citadel of Susa,
Hanani, one of my brothers, arrived with men from Judah. So I questioned them about the {{sheerith|remnant}} of the Jews who had survived the exile, and also about Jerusalem.
And they told me, “The remnant who survived the exile are there in the province, in great trouble and disgrace. The wall of Jerusalem is broken down, and its gates have been burned with fire.”
When I heard these words, I sat down and wept. I mourned for days, fasting and praying before the God of heaven.

units.label: The God who keeps covenant
units.note: great and awesome, keeping loving devotion

verses.text: Then I said: “O LORD, God of heaven, the great and awesome God who keeps His {{berith|covenant}} of {{chesed|loving devotion}} with those who love Him and keep His commandments,

let Your eyes be open and Your ears attentive to hear the prayer that I, Your servant, now pray before You day and night for Your servants, the Israelites. I confess the sins that we Israelites have committed against You. Both I and my father's house have sinned.
We have behaved corruptly against You and have not kept the commandments, statutes, and ordinances that You gave Your servant Moses.

units.label: Remember Your word, grant success
units.note: the promise to Moses and the plea before the king

verses.text: Remember, I pray, the word that You commanded Your servant Moses when You said, ‘If you are {{maal|unfaithful}}, I will scatter you among the nations,

but if you return to Me and keep and practice My commandments, then even if your exiles have been banished to the farthest horizon, I will gather them from there and bring them to the place I have chosen as a dwelling for My Name.’
They are Your servants and Your people. You {{padah|redeemed}} them by Your great power and mighty hand.

O Lord, may Your ear be attentive to my prayer and to the prayers of Your servants who delight to {{yare|revere}} Your name. Give Your servant success this day, I pray, and grant him mercy in the sight of this man.” (At that time I was the cupbearer to the king.)

sheerith.gloss: A remnant, those left over, the survivors. Nehemiah asks first about the sheerith, the fragile company that outlived the exile. The word carries both grief and hope: the nation was nearly wiped out, yet God has kept a remnant alive, and it is for these survivors that Nehemiah weeps and prays.

berith.gloss: Covenant, a solemn binding bond. Nehemiah appeals to the God who keeps His berith, the sworn relationship God made with His people. His whole prayer rests here: not on Israel's record, which he confesses is broken, but on God's own unbreakable commitment to His covenant word.

chesed.gloss: Loving devotion, steadfast covenant love. The BSB footnote notes its range: love, goodness, kindness, faithfulness, mercy, and loyalty to a covenant. Chesed is the loyal love that keeps promises even when the other party fails. Nehemiah pleads that this God keeps covenant and chesed together, so a sinful people may still hope in Him.

maal.gloss: Unfaithfulness, treachery, a breach of trust against God. In the word to Moses that Nehemiah recalls, maal is the covenant betrayal that brought the scattering. Naming the exile as the fruit of unfaithfulness, Nehemiah owns that the ruin was deserved, which makes God's promise to gather the returning penitent all the more gracious.

padah.gloss: To redeem, to ransom, to buy back by paying a price. Nehemiah reminds God that these are the people He padah, redeemed, by His great power and mighty hand, language drawn from the exodus. The plea is that the God who once ransomed His people at such cost will not now abandon them.

yare.gloss: To fear, to revere, to hold in awe. Nehemiah numbers himself among the servants who delight to yare God's name, a reverent fear that is not terror but glad worship. It marks out the true covenant people: not the flawless, but those whose hearts are set to honour and obey the LORD.

basic.context: The book of Nehemiah opens with its hero far from home. Nehemiah is a Jewish exile serving as cupbearer, a trusted royal official, in the Persian citadel of Susa. His brother Hanani arrives with news from Judah, and it is devastating: the survivors of the exile are in great trouble and disgrace, and the wall of Jerusalem is still broken down, its gates burned. This is decades after the first exiles returned and the temple was rebuilt, yet the holy city lies exposed and shamed. Nehemiah's response is the key to the whole book. He does not rush into planning or lobbying. He sits down and weeps, mourns for days, and fasts and prays. The question the chapter presses is: when we hear of something broken that we long to see put right, where do we begin? Nehemiah begins on his knees.
basic.hermeneutics: This is narrative, and Nehemiah's prayer is a model, not a magic formula. We are not told to copy his exact words, but we are shown the shape of faithful prayer: it begins with who God is, moves to honest confession, leans on God's promises, and then asks boldly for specific help. Nehemiah prays about a city wall, but the deeper concern is the honour of God's name and the good of God's people. The enduring truth is that real work for God is soaked in prayer from the start, and that we approach God not on the basis of our record but on the basis of His covenant faithfulness.
basic.originalLanguages: Nehemiah asks first about the sheerith, the remnant, the survivors God has kept alive. He addresses the God who keeps His berith, His covenant, and His chesed, His loving devotion, the loyal love that keeps promises even when His people fail. He recalls that the exile came through maal, unfaithfulness, so the ruin was deserved. Yet he reminds God that these are the people He padah, redeemed, at great cost, and he stands among those who yare, revere, God's name.
basic.typology: Nehemiah, safe in the king's palace, is moved to grief over a ruined city and its scattered people, and he steps forward to intercede and, later, to go down and rebuild. In this he is a shadow of Christ, who left the courts of heaven, was moved with compassion over a lost people, and came down to rebuild what sin had ruined. Nehemiah's confession of a sin he did not personally commit, owning it as we and our, points to Jesus, who was numbered with transgressors and carried a guilt that was not His own.
basic.christ: Nehemiah stands between a holy God and a guilty people, confessing their sin as his own and pleading God's mercy on their behalf. He is an intercessor, and every true intercessor points to the one perfect Intercessor, Jesus, who ever lives to pray for us. Nehemiah appeals to God's covenant and to a past redemption by mighty hand; we appeal to a greater covenant sealed in Christ's blood and a greater redemption won at the cross. And Nehemiah's cry that God would move a pagan king to show mercy foreshadows the God who rules every heart and moved history itself toward the coming of His Son.
basic.god: God is revealed as the great and awesome God of heaven, sovereign over Persia and its king, and yet near enough to bend His ear to a weeping exile. He keeps covenant and loving devotion together: He is faithful to His promises and rich in loyal love, even to a people who confess they have sinned. He is the God who scatters the unfaithful in justice but gathers the returning penitent in mercy, exactly as He promised Moses. This is a God worth praying to, because His character, not our performance, is the ground of hope.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 30:1-4 is the very promise Nehemiah pleads: if the scattered return, God will gather them. Daniel 9 offers a strikingly similar prayer of confession over the same exile. Hebrews 7:25 shows Christ as the intercessor who always lives to pray for His people. And Proverbs 21:1 declares that the king's heart is in the LORD's hand, the truth behind Nehemiah's plea for success before the king.
basic.oneStory: The exile left God's people scattered and their city in ruins, and the great story of Scripture is God's faithfulness to gather and restore them for the sake of His name. Nehemiah's prayer stands at the head of one chapter in that story, resting the whole rebuilding on God's covenant loving devotion. It points forward to Christ, who came down to rebuild a far greater ruin, gathering a people from every nation and making them a dwelling for God's Name.

context.literary: The chapter is the doorway to the whole book, moving from a dated report to Nehemiah's grief to an extended prayer that frames everything that follows. It is written as first person memoir, giving it an intimate, diary-like quality. The prayer itself is carefully built: invocation, confession, appeal to promise, and petition, echoing the covenant language of Deuteronomy and the great confessional prayers of the exile. The closing note, that Nehemiah was cupbearer to the king, is held back to the last line, setting up the drama of the next chapter.
context.historical: The events unfold in Susa, a Persian royal capital, in the twentieth year of Artaxerxes, around the middle of the fifth century before Christ. As cupbearer, Nehemiah held a position of great trust and access, tasting the king's wine against poison and standing near the throne. The temple in Jerusalem had been rebuilt decades earlier under Zerubbabel and Ezra, but the city's wall lay in ruins, leaving the returned community defenceless, vulnerable to hostile neighbours, and covered in disgrace in a world where a walled city meant security and honour.
context.canonical: Nehemiah continues the story of Ezra, with which it was once joined as a single book, chronicling the restoration of the post-exilic community. Nehemiah's prayer stands alongside the great corporate confessions of Daniel 9 and Nehemiah 9 and Ezra 9, all owning the nation's guilt and appealing to God's mercy. Its plea rests on the promise of Deuteronomy 30, and its theme, God gathering a scattered people to a place He has chosen for His Name, runs on to its fulfilment in Christ, who gathers a people from every nation into the true dwelling of God.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that the restoration of God's city and people began not with strategy or resources but with grief, fasting, and believing prayer, and to model an approach to God grounded in His covenant character, honest confession, His own promises, and bold, specific petition.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The chapter describes how Nehemiah responded to a particular crisis in a particular time; it does not prescribe his exact words or the wall project as a pattern for every situation. What endures is the shape of his praying, beginning with who God is, confessing sin honestly, pleading God's promises, and asking boldly, and the conviction that work for God should be soaked in prayer before it is soaked in effort.

debates.question: Why does Nehemiah confess sins he did not personally commit?

views.label: Genuine solidarity in corporate guilt
views.case: Nehemiah sees himself as truly part of one covenant people; his 'we' and 'our' and his admission that 'I and my father's house have sinned' express real solidarity, refusing to stand above his community or treat its guilt as someone else's problem.

views.label: Representative intercession
views.case: As one seeking to act on the nation's behalf, Nehemiah takes the people's guilt onto his own lips in prayer, standing before God as a mediator for the whole community rather than distancing himself from its sin.

debates.bearing: Both readings converge, and either way Nehemiah's identification with the guilty foreshadows Christ, who, though sinless, was numbered with transgressors and made His people's cause His own before God.

debates.question: What is Nehemiah really asking for when he prays for success and mercy before the king?

views.label: A specific request about approaching Artaxerxes
views.case: Nehemiah already has a plan forming to ask the king for leave to rebuild; 'this man' is the king, and he prays that God would grant him favour in the very conversation he is about to risk, which unfolds in chapter 2.

views.label: A trusting appeal to God's sovereignty over rulers
views.case: The petition rests on the conviction that the king's heart is in God's hand; Nehemiah asks not merely for courage but for God to move a pagan monarch, entrusting the outcome to the God of heaven who rules over Persia.

debates.bearing: Both are true together: Nehemiah prays a concrete, risky request while resting it wholly on God's rule over kings, showing faith that is specific and dependent at once.

study.originalLanguages: The prayer is woven from covenant vocabulary. Nehemiah's first concern is the sheerith, the remnant, the survivors God has kept from the wreckage of exile, a word heavy with both loss and hope. He grounds his appeal in the God who keeps His berith, His covenant, the sworn bond that holds firm when His people do not, and His chesed, His loving devotion, the loyal, promise-keeping love whose range runs from kindness and mercy to sheer covenant faithfulness. He does not soften the reason for the ruin: the exile came through maal, unfaithfulness, covenant treachery, so the scattering was deserved. Yet against that dark honesty he sets God's own acts, reminding Him that these are the people He padah, redeemed, ransomed by great power and a mighty hand, the language of the exodus. And he names the company he belongs to, the servants who yare, who revere and delight to honour God's name, a reverent awe that is worship, not terror. Remnant, covenant, loving devotion, unfaithfulness, redemption, reverence: the words trace the whole arc from guilt to grace.

typology.type: Nehemiah confessing the nation's guilt as his own
typology.fulfillment: Christ, though sinless, numbered with the transgressors and bearing our guilt before God, Isaiah 53:12 and 2 Corinthians 5:21.

typology.type: Nehemiah interceding for a broken people and then coming down to rebuild
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who left heaven's courts, was moved with compassion, and came down to rebuild what sin had ruined, gathering a scattered people to God.

typology.type: The God who gathers exiles to the place chosen for His Name
typology.fulfillment: Christ, who gathers a people from every nation into the true temple, the dwelling of God's Name, John 11:52 and Ephesians 2:19-22.

study.christ: Nehemiah stands between a holy God and a guilty people, confessing their sin as if it were his own and pleading for mercy on their behalf. In this he is a living picture of the intercessor, and every intercessor points to the one perfect Mediator, the Lord Jesus, who ever lives to pray for us. Nehemiah appeals to God's covenant and to a past redemption by mighty hand; the believer now appeals to a greater covenant sealed in Christ's own blood and a greater redemption won not by the arm of the exodus but by the cross. Nehemiah, secure in a palace, is broken over a ruined city and will soon leave his comfort to go down and rebuild it, a shadow of the Son who left the glory of heaven, was moved with compassion over a lost and scattered people, and came down to raise up what sin had razed. And his plea that God would move the heart of a pagan king to mercy rests on the same sovereign Lord who governed all of history to bring His Son into the world at the appointed time.
study.god: God is revealed here as the great and awesome God of heaven, high above the Persian empire and its king, and yet attentive to the tears of a single exile who mourns for days. He keeps covenant and loving devotion together, holding His justice and His mercy in one hand: He scatters the unfaithful, exactly as He warned, yet stands ready to gather the returning penitent, exactly as He promised. He is faithful when His people are faithless, bound not by their record but by His own sworn word and His loyal love. This is the God of the gospel, whose grace is real because His justice is real, and whose covenant faithfulness, proven in Israel's history and sealed in Christ, is the sure ground on which guilty people may still come to Him in hope.

crossReferences.note: The promise to Moses that Nehemiah pleads: if the scattered people return to the LORD, He will gather them even from the farthest horizon.
A parallel prayer of corporate confession over the same exile, owning the nation's guilt and appealing to God's great mercy.
The king's heart is a stream in the LORD's hand, the truth behind Nehemiah's plea for mercy in the sight of the king.
Christ, the perfect intercessor, always lives to make intercession for His people, fulfilling the pattern Nehemiah embodies.

study.oneStory: The exile scattered God's people and left His city in ruins, and the deep current of Scripture is God's faithfulness to gather and restore them for the honour of His name. Nehemiah's prayer opens one chapter of that story, resting the whole rebuilding not on his own resolve but on God's covenant loving devotion, His promise to Moses, and His past redemption of His people. It points beyond the wall of Jerusalem to Christ, who came down to rebuild a far greater ruin, bearing the guilt of His people, gathering them from every nation, and making them the living dwelling of God's Name.

quiz.q: What news moved Nehemiah to weep, fast, and pray?

options.text: That the survivors of the exile were in disgrace and Jerusalem's wall was broken down and its gates burned.
options.note: Verses 2-4 report the ruined wall and the disgrace of the remnant, and Nehemiah's grief.

options.text: That the temple in Jerusalem had just been destroyed.
options.note: The temple had been rebuilt decades earlier; the crisis was the ruined wall and the people's disgrace.

options.text: That the king of Persia had ordered the Jews killed.
options.note: No such order is mentioned; the report concerned Jerusalem's broken wall and the remnant's trouble.

options.text: That Nehemiah had lost his position as cupbearer.
options.note: Nehemiah was still cupbearer; his grief was for Jerusalem, not his own standing.

quiz.q: How does Nehemiah begin his prayer?

options.text: By addressing God as the great and awesome God who keeps His covenant of loving devotion.
options.note: Verse 5 grounds the whole prayer in God's covenant character and loyal love.

options.text: By listing his own good works to earn a hearing.
options.note: The opposite: Nehemiah appeals to God's character and confesses sin, not his merit.

options.text: By demanding that God punish the enemies of Jerusalem.
options.note: He begins with who God is and confession, not with calls for vengeance.

options.text: By presenting his building plans to God.
options.note: He begins with worship and confession; the request comes only at the end.

quiz.q: What does Nehemiah confess in his prayer?

options.text: The sins of Israel as his own, saying both he and his father's house have sinned.
options.note: Verses 6-7 show Nehemiah owning the nation's guilt corporately, though he is the one praying.

options.text: That only the previous generation was to blame for the exile.
options.note: He does not shift blame; he includes himself and his own house in the confession.

options.text: That the exile was entirely undeserved.
options.note: He confesses real unfaithfulness and corrupt behaviour, not innocence.

options.text: That he had no idea why the exile happened.
options.note: He knows and names the cause: the people broke God's commandments.

quiz.q: On what basis does Nehemiah appeal for God to act?

options.text: God's own promise to Moses to gather His people if they return, and His past redemption of them.
options.note: Verses 8-10 plead God's covenant word and His mighty redemption, not Israel's worthiness.

options.text: The strength of the Jewish army to defend the city.
options.note: There was no army; Nehemiah appeals to God's promise and power, not military strength.

options.text: His own righteousness as a faithful cupbearer.
options.note: He appeals to God's promise and redemption, not to his personal merit.

options.text: A bargain in which Israel would repay God later.
options.note: He pleads grace and covenant, not a transaction Israel could fulfil.

quiz.q: What does Nehemiah ask for at the close of his prayer, and how does it point to Christ?

options.text: Success and mercy before the king, resting on God's rule over rulers, foreshadowing Christ the intercessor who moves history for His people.
options.note: Verse 11 asks for favour before the king; Nehemiah's intercession points to Christ who ever lives to pray for us.

options.text: That God would let him stay comfortably in Susa forever.
options.note: The opposite: he prays for success in a risky venture, ready to leave his comfort.

options.text: That the king would be overthrown by force.
options.note: He prays for mercy in the king's sight, trusting God to move the king's heart, not for a coup.

options.text: That God would leave the ruined city as it was.
options.note: His whole prayer longs for restoration, not for the ruin to remain.

questions: Nehemiah was so moved by a broken city that he wept and fasted for days before doing anything else. What burden has God laid on your heart lately, and does it drive you first to prayer or straight to activity?
Before asking for anything, Nehemiah dwelt on who God is: great, awesome, and faithful to His covenant loving devotion. How might beginning your prayers with God's character change the way you pray?

Nehemiah confessed the nation's sin as his own, refusing to stand above his people. Where might God be calling you to identify with and intercede for your church, family, or nation rather than only criticising it?
Nehemiah prayed God's own promise back to Him. Which of God's promises do you most need to hold on to and plead before Him right now?

Nehemiah trusted that the heart of the most powerful man he knew was in God's hand. What situation feels beyond your control, and how would it change things to entrust it to the God of heaven who rules over kings?

translationNotes.note: Chislev is the ninth month of the Hebrew lunar calendar, falling roughly within November and December; the twentieth year refers to the reign of Artaxerxes, named later in the book.

Covenant of loving devotion renders the Hebrew chesed, whose range, per the BSB footnote, includes love, goodness, kindness, faithfulness, mercy, and loyalty to a covenant; it is God's steadfast, promise-keeping love.

Banished to the farthest horizon can also be rendered to the extremity of the heavens; the promise Nehemiah cites draws on Deuteronomy 30:1-4, that God will gather even the most distant exiles who return to Him.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Nehemiah 1:1-11; each verse assembled by concatenating its content segments in order and ignoring headings, line breaks, and note markers. Chapter 1 is Hebrew throughout. No em or en dashes present or introduced; dashes converted to commas or clean breaks.
